coredata.dc:title: Leachate xenobiotics electrocatalytic degradation and simultaneous carbon quantum dots synthesis for anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadarshini Rajesh, R.
Saravanakumar, M.P.
coredata.dc:description: The leachate treatment and the management of sludge after its treatment is a big challenge due to its many toxins and pollutants. The novelty of the current work resides in our attempt to address both difficulties by treating the leachate with electrofenton using low-cost stainless steel electrodes, followed by converting the sludge generated into quantum dots suited for anti-counterfeiting applications. On the electrofenton treatment of leachate, at the optimized conditions, the percentage removal of total organic carbon (TOC), UV254, chemical oxygen demand (COD), and NH3-N was found to be 79 %, 93 %, 90 %, and 90 % respectively with less energy consumption of 0.26 KWh/KgCOD. The gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS), 3D fluorescence spectroscopy matrix, and UV–Vis analysis confirmed the removal of xenobiotics and dissolved organic matter (DOM). The carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) synthesized from sludge were characterized for X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), PL intensity (Photoluminescence), and UV–Vis. The S-CQDs are very promising in qualitative fingerprint image enhancement in terms of overall pattern, dots, and ridges and are well suited as an alternate, efficient, green anticounterfeiting material. Thus, highly polluted leachate was efficiently treated and sludge was converted into useful carbon dots with the anti-counterfeiting application.

head.ce:title: Leachate xenobiotics electrocatalytic degradation and simultaneous carbon quantum dots synthesis for anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental and Water Resources Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632 014 Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental and Water Resources Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental and Water Resources Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632 014 Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The leachate treatment and the management of sludge after its treatment is a big challenge due to its many toxins and pollutants. The novelty of the current work resides in our attempt to address both difficulties by treating the leachate with electrofenton using low-cost stainless steel electrodes, followed by converting the sludge generated into quantum dots suited for anti-counterfeiting applications. On the electrofenton treatment of leachate, at the optimized conditions, the percentage removal of total organic carbon (TOC), UV254, chemical oxygen demand (COD), and NH3-N was found to be 79 %, 93 %, 90 %, and 90 % respectively with less energy consumption of 0.26 KWh/KgCOD. The gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS), 3D fluorescence spectroscopy matrix, and UV–Vis analysis confirmed the removal of xenobiotics and dissolved organic matter (DOM). The carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) synthesized from sludge were characterized for X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), PL intensity (Photoluminescence), and UV–Vis. The S-CQDs are very promising in qualitative fingerprint image enhancement in terms of overall pattern, dots, and ridges and are well suited as an alternate, efficient, green anticounterfeiting material. Thus, highly polluted leachate was efficiently treated and sludge was converted into useful carbon dots with the anti-counterfeiting application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Should any raw data files be needed will be made available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:para:
The landfills generate leachate which causes a serious impact on human health and the ecosystem if improperly treated and disposed of [1]. The pollutants mostly belong to the 126 priority pollutants defined by the United States Environmental Protection Agency [2]. It is also expected to have few undiscovered and unpredictable pollutants [3]. Many physicochemical and biological methods are attempted for the treatment of leachate [4]. Among them, electrochemical oxidation has grasped the interest of researchers in the last two decades because of its wide application [5–8], less environmental footprint, simple design as well as operation, automation facility, degradation of refractory organics with toxic pollutants, low sludge production, and complete mineralization [9–13]. Because of its advantages, electrochemical technologies have found a niche where they can become dominant soon. The electrochemical oxidation reaction occurs in two ways namely direct and indirect electrooxidation [14]. In direct oxidation, pollutants get adsorbed to the anode surface and degraded by an anodic electron transfer reaction. This phenomenon occurs either in single or multiple stages and continues till the final oxidation product mostly CO2 is produced [15]. The indirect oxidation occurs at the anode surface to form active chlorine (gaseous chlorine, hypochlorous acid, or hypochlorite) from chloride ions present in the landfill leachate contributing to TOC removal [16].
(1) 2Cl- → Cl2 + 2e-
(2) Cl2 + H2O → HOCl + H+ + Cl-
(3) HOCl + R → CO2 + H2O + H+ + Cl-

Many types of electrochemical oxidation methods have been reported for the treatment of leachate among which electrofenton has proved to be efficient in removing refractory organics in leachate with less sludge production [17]. The electrofenton mechanism can be given by the following equations.
(4) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH– + HO
(5) Fe3+ + H2O2⟶Fe2+ + H+ + HO2
(6) Fe3+ + HO2 •⟶Fe2+ + O2 + H+
(7) O2 + 2H+ + 2e-⟶ H2O2
(8) 2H2O → O2 + 4H+ + 4e-

In most of the electrofenton treatments, the anodes were usually boron-doped diamond [18] or metal oxide coated [19]. Stainless steel electrodes were used only for the electrocoagulation of different industrial effluents [20]. This work attempts to determine the suitability of stainless steel electrodes for leachate electrofenton treatment. Stainless steel offers advantages like corrosion resistance, commercially available in different constitutions, stability, low cost, and ease of handling [21]. The parameters like pH, applied voltage, inter-electrode distance, Fe2+ dosage, H2O2 dosage and electrolysis time have been optimized. The FTIR analysis is done to identify the functional group existence of different organic and inorganic substances in leachate. The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectroscopy has been used to analyze the DOM removal in the fenton and electrofenton treatment of leachate. GC–MS analysis is done to evaluate the toxins, carcinogens removal, and by-product formation in electrofenton treatment.

The Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have demonstrated outstanding photostability, highly regulated photoluminescence, biocompatibility, electrochemiluminescence, biocompatibility, and an extraordinary aptitude for multi-photon excitation (up-conversion) since their surprising discovery in 2006 [22]. CQDs can typically be synthesized using both top-down and bottom-up techniques [23–26]. Top-down procedures relate to processes that involve fragmenting big carbon structures into smaller pieces, such as chemical oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, arc discharge, laser ablation, and so on [27]. CQDs are generated in these techniques from macroscopic carbon materials such as graphite, active carbon, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, coal, carbonized waste carbon paper, and others. Bottom-up approaches refer to the creation of CQDs from organic molecule precursors through the use of specialized synthetic methods such as microwave irradiation, hydrothermal treatments, ultrasonication, and thermal combustion [28]. Exploring easy, affordable, and environmentally benign ways to synthesize CQDs is critical for large-scale commercialization [29].

Researchers have paid a lot of attention to the continuously growing sludge problem since it has become a major environmental issue on a global scale. Utilizing sludge as a renewable resource, such as by looking into ways to turn usable components in sludge into valuable commodities, is therefore extremely important and urgent [30]. Incineration is the common methodology to dispose of sludge which includes problems of further processing of incineration residues, higher O & M costs, and processing of hazardous sludge [31]. The useful material production from the sludge will increase the life cycle of the product in a bio-circular economy by reducing its carbon footprint. Many products such as biochar, activated sludge, construction materials, adsorbents, etc., were produced from the sludge and were in practical applications throughout the world [32].

Very few studies have been attempted for the conversion of waste-generated products into quantum dots [29,33]. In this study, the S-CQDs were successfully synthesized from the sludge derived from the electrofenton treatment using the hydrothermal method and it has been characterized for FTIR, XRD, TEM, UV–Vis, and PL. Many applications of CQDs such as bio-imaging, bio-sensing [24], photodynamic cancer therapy [23], fluorescent probes [34], anti-counterfeiting [35], etc., prepared from various sources have been reported. The comparison table for the synthesis of quantum dots from various sources of waste with different applications, quantum yield, etc., is given in Table 3 .

The synthesized S-CQDs were successfully applied to fingerprint development for anti-counterfeiting applications. So much research work has been going on for the proper detection of fingerprints in the forensic department replacing the classical methods. Fluorescent powders outperform normal powders in terms of concealing colors and patterns on surfaces, and fingerprints created with them can fluoresce under certain light. Several fluorescent micro or nanomaterials have been investigated as potential fingerprint powders over the last few decades [36]. The S-CQDs powder was applied to the latent fingerprint, excited under UV light, and studied for point of identification, ridge, and pattern stability. The S-CQDs fluorescent powder was found to be a good alternative to the traditional black powder in fingerprint development.

The leachate was collected from the open dump site located near Vellore City, Tamilnadu, India. For the past 50 years, municipal solid waste has been dumped here [37]. After collecting leachate from the dumpsite, it was immediately transported and stored in the laboratory below 4◦C. All chemicals used in this study like Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 % w/v), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98 % w/w), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98 %), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O, 99 %) of analytical grade and purchased from SD fine chemical Limited, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India. Deionized water from the Millipore Direct-Ultrapure water system (MilliQ) was used for the analysis. The two Stainless steel plates used as electrodes were of type-304 standardized according to ISO 3506 of the Jindal brand with the composition of 0.07 % Carbon, 2 % Manganese, 0.03 % Sulfur, 0.045 % Phosphorous, 0.75 % Silicon, 10.5 % Nickel, 19.5 % Chromium, and 0.10 % Nitrogen [64].

The electrofenton reaction was carried out in a one-litre glass beaker with 500 ml leachate taken for each experimental trial. Two rectangular Stainless steel rods, (20 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.6 cm each) one as cathode and the other as anode with a total effective contact area of 17.5 cm2 immersed in leachate. Normal aeration of air at 3 L/min was provided near the cathode area. The reactor setup is given in Fig. 1
. The leachate was adjusted for pH initially with 5 N H2SO4 or 5 N NaOH. Then, the required amount of iron salt was added and mixed. After 5 min, hydrogen peroxide was added and mixed. The power supply and aerator were switched on parallelly after the addition of chemicals. Appropriate leachate samples were collected periodically during the analysis treatment. All experiments were performed at room temperature (26⁰ C). The electrodes were washed immediately after the experiments to avoid any rust formation.

The S-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method [23,24,34] using an autoclave. The wet sludge was collected and dried in a hot air oven at 60 °C till all the moisture was removed. In a 100 ml Teflon autoclave tube, 5 g of dried sample and, 75 ml of MilliQ water were added and packed in an autoclave made of Stainless-steel. It is kept in a hot air oven for 7 h at 180 °C. After that, it is taken out and cooled to room temperature (26 °C). The dark brown solution obtained was filtered using a 0.22 μm PVDF membrane filter and subjected to dialysis for 24 h for further purification. The liquid left in the dialysis membrane was freeze-dried to obtain the pure quantum dots. The powder was sieved in a 0.15 mm sieve and pulverized using mortar & pestle to obtain fine and smooth powder. The synthesis procedure was detailed in Fig. 7. The unreacted remained sludge was converted into biochar by keeping it in a muffle furnace at 600 °C for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of S-CQDs for anti-counterfeiting applications
ce:section.ce:para:
The fingerprint donor washed her hands with handwash and dried them neatly before keeping the impression. She rubbed her thumb finger with the sweat from her forehead and kept her impression with medium force on the white paper which is preferably used for fingerprint identification. The S-CQDs black powder is spread on the fingerprint using a smooth fingerprint brush and excess powder is swept away mildly by the tip of the brush. Then the image is captured under normal and UV light at 365 nm. The procedure of fingerprint development is represented in Fig. S5
.

The pH was determined using a digital pH meter (Systronics-335). A Vandelay Infrared Thermometer was used to measure temperature. The COD sample was incubated in a closed digester DRB200 digital block. The turbidity was determined using a digital turbidity meter (Environmental and Scientific Instruments, model 335). The aerator of model BL-108 Power, 3 L/min is used for aeration. The chemicals were precisely weighed using an electronic balance (Shimadzu AUW220 UniBloc). UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hach USA DR6000) was used to measure UV absorbance and NH3-N. The total carbon (TC), organic carbon (OC), and inorganic carbon (IC) were measured using a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu, TOC-L series), which was based on the combustion catalytic oxidation method (680 °C). Conductivity was determined using a digital conductivity meter (systronics MK-509). Model GW INSTEK GPS-4303 was used as the DC power supply. The 3D fluorescence scan was examined using a 3D excitation-emission matrix (EEM, Hitachi Fluorescence Spectrophotometer-F7000) under the condition of applied voltage 450 V, xenon arc lamp with 150 W, scanning speed of 12000 nm/min, scanning interval 5 nm, slit width 5 nm, pathlength of quartz cuvette 1 cm and both excitation & emission wavelength range between 200 and 600 nm. The PL analysis of S-CQDs was also measured using this instrument. The metal analysis was performed using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Avio 200). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were performed using an FTIR spectrometer (IR Affinity-1, Shimadzu). The XRD analysis was done using the model Bruker D8 Advance. The morphology of CQDs was obtained using transmission electron microscopy, (TEM, JEOL 3010). The samples were dried in a hot air oven (IL-AO-3000E). A sonicator (Elmasonic P) was used to conduct ultrasonication of CQDs. Remi magnetic stirrer of 2MLH was used for the dialysis process. Dialysis membrane-70 with 500 Da to 1000 Da cut-off, Himedia was used for dialysis purification. The leachate samples preparation for GC–MS analysis and analysis conditions were given in the supplementary. COD (5220C), BOD (5210B), chlorides, (4500B), total solids (2540 A), and ammoniacal nitrogen (4500F) determination have been done according to APHA [61]. The dissolved organic matter (DOM) is measured by filtering the sample through a 0.45 µm filter followed by TOC analysis. The SUVA is calculated by dividing the UV absorbance of the sample (in cm−1) by the DOM of the sample (in mg/L) and then multiplying by 100 cm/M. SUVA is reported in units of L/mg-M. S-CQDs excitation and its optical absorption were measured on a UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan).

The removal efficiency of UV254, TOC, COD, etc., of all parameters was determined by the following equation [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of parameters of treatment of leachate pollutant

ce:section.ce:para:
The pH value also has an important influence on electromigration, which determines the electrooxidation process efficiency [42]. The pH of the raw leachate was 8.9 revealing that the leachate was matured which was again confirmed by its dark brown color and the low BOD/COD ratio [43]. The characteristics of raw leachate and treated samples at optimized conditions are given in Table 1 . The effect of variation of pH was studied at pH values 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 at the condition of 80 mM Fe2 + dosage, 1.8 g/L H2O2 dosage, 2 cm electrode 1, and 4.5 V. The TOC and UV254 removal efficiency tends to decrease when the pH value increases. From the graph, (Fig. 2
(a)) it was obvious that at 3 pH maximum TOC removal was obtained. The DOM removal at various pH was represented in Fig. S3 . At pH 9, some DOM removal was found which is due to the occurrence of an electrocoagulation-like reaction. Acidic condition enhances the TOC removal by forming more •OH and HClO•/ClO•. A higher pH value enhances the electrocoagulation reaction where the sludge formation is more [44]. It was also observed that the pH value tends to increase when the reaction time increases. This is because of the formation of hydrogen at the cathode leading to a decrease of H+ ions concentration. The Fig. 3
(d), shows the change in pH value as the reaction proceeds at the optimized dosage viz 3 pH, 80 mM Fe2+, 1.8 g/L H2O2, and at 4.5 V. The change in pH value of a few reactions with different voltages and dosages during the reaction time was given in Table ST1 . At higher voltages, the pH value increases rapidly during the electrooxidation. At 7.5 V, 80 mM Fe2 + dosage, 4.2 g/L H2O2 dosage, 2 cm electrode distance, 15 min of reaction time, the pH drops to 5.23 and in one hour to 6.52. Only changing the pH of raw leachate to the level of 3 pH has shown TOC removal to nearly 20 % and UV254 removal to 67 %. It is due to the organic precipitation occurring at pH 3 [2]. However, the DOM has not been removed by only changing the value to 3 pH which is represented in Fig. S3
(e).

The Fe2+ dosage was varied for 0, 40, 80, and 120 mM at 3 pH, 1.8 g/L H2O2, 2 cm electrode distance, and 4.5 V represented in Fig. 2 (b) and observed for TOC and UV254 removal. At zero iron external dosage, no TOC removal was found. Within the first 30 min of reaction time. After 180 min of reaction time, the TOC removal was almost nearly 55 %. The DOM EEM contour results given in Fig. S1 also represent the same. This shows that H2O2 addition alone is not sufficient for the maximum removal of TOC. The increase in Fe dosage increased the TOC removal efficiency. Because the iron catalyzes the H2O2 generation thereby increasing the •OH radical production [45]. Fig. 2 (b) shows that the maximum removal of TOC and UV254 was at 80 mM Fe2+ dosage. The increase in iron dosage from 80 mM to 120 mM, slightly decreases the TOC removal percentage. This was due to the precipitation of Fe(OH)3 at the cathode [46].

The H2O2 dosage was varied for 0, 0.6, 1.8, 3, and 4.2 g/L at the condition of 3 pH, 80 mM Fe2+, 2 cm electrode distance, 4.5 V, and the results are given in Fig. 2 (d). At zero external dose of H2O2, the TOC removal efficiency was very less but after 240 min it reached nearly 75 %. At 30 min, it was 32 %, at 60 min it was 50 %, at 120 min it was 68 % and at 180 min it was 74 %. After 120 min, the TOC removal rate was meager. This was also confirmed by the DOM EEM contours in Fig. S2 . If run time and electricity consumption were not a concern, say for example treatment plants operating with solar energy, we can avoid the external addition of H2O2 thereby increasing the electrooxidation treatment time reducing overall operational cost. However, the external addition of H2O2 increases the TOC removal rate. At 0.6 and 1.8 g/L of H2O2 dosage, the TOC removal was 46 % and 79 % at 30 min of reaction time. Even after increasing the dosage to 3 g/L at 30 min the TOC removal was only 83 % and at 4.2 g/L of H2O2, it slightly decreased to 81 %. Hence, we can conclude that 1.8 g/L was the optimized H2O2 dosage. The surplus addition of H2O2 leads to the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2 reducing the •OH radical production thereby reducing the organics removal rate [47].
(14) 2H2O2 → 2H2O + O2
(15) •OH + •OH → H2O2

The influence of voltage on the electrochemical reaction was observed. The voltage has been varied viz 1.5 V, 3 V, 4.5 V, 6 V, and 7.5 V at 3 pH, 80 mM Fe2+, 2 cm electrode distance, and 1.8 g/L H2O2 dosage. Once the power supply was given, flocculation formation was more for the first 30 min and almost 50 ml of leachate out of 500 ml was going as flocs. Less flocculation was found after 30 min. At higher voltages, much higher flocculation was found than at lower voltages. The removal of organics occurs through more consumption of electricity with higher flocs [3]. The TOC removal rate was also higher in the first 30 min. From Fig. 2 (c), it was obvious that increasing the voltage increases the TOC and UV254 removal. At 4.5 V the TOC removal was 79 % for 30 min and it has been increased slightly to 83 % at 6 V and reduced to 80 % at 7.5 V. The UV254 removal was 90 % at 4.5 V after 30 min of treatment. Further, the increase after 4.5 V decreases the TOC removal may be due to the formation of unreacted chlorine byproducts and evaporation of chlorine gas. The efficiency may also be decreased due to the undesired side reaction of O2 formation at the anode and H2 formation at the cathode [48]. Hence, we can conclude that 4.5 V is the optimized voltage. Higher voltage favors indirect oxidation thereby generating more •OH radicals and the formation of other oxidants like hydrogen peroxide, chlorine, peroxophosphate, persulfate, etc., [3,46,49]. As the experiment proceeded, the sludge formation at the bottom was noticed. Till 120 min, there was no sludge formation but after 120 min sludge formation began and it was more after 180 min nearly 100 ml formed as sludge out of 500 ml of leachate after 240 min of reaction time. The leachate became pale yellow in 30 min of reaction time and became almost colorless after 240 min of treatment. Visible small bubbles were continuously formed from the anode throughout the reaction.

The distance between the electrodes has been varied for 1 cm, 2 cm, 3 cm, and 4 cm at 3 pH, 80 mM Fe2+, 1.8 g/L H2O2, and 4.5 V. From the Fig. 2 (e), it was obvious that 2 cm was the ideal distance between the electrodes. When the distance between the electrodes was decreased, the cell resistance decreased and current flow increased, obeying the property of Ohm’s law [50]. Reducing the inter-electrode distance may decrease the mass transfer limitations of the contaminants towards the electrode surface and enhance their electrochemical transformation [51]. Also, when the electrodes are placed too close, the electro-regenerated ferrous ion gets easily oxidized to a ferric ion at the anode, reducing the efficiency of electrofenton reactions.
(16) Fe2+→ Fe3+ + e−

Longer distance attributed to limited mass transfer of ferric ions to the cathode surface where ferrous ion regeneration occurs. Therefore, the hydroxyl radicals formed could not act efficiently from ferrous ion regeneration [52]. The role of aeration, chloride concentration, and conductivity is given in the supplementary information.

The FTIR spectrum for the raw leachate and the treated leachate at the optimized condition of 3 pH, 80 mM Fe2+, 1.8 g/L H2O2, 4.5 V and with various timings is shown in Fig. 3 (b). The 3310 cm−1 short stretching was due to O–H vibrations of carboxylic acids. The peak at 1716 cm−1 was because of the CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid or amide group. The peak 1343 cm−1 can be assigned to the NO2 stretch indicating the presence of nitrite components. The peak at 1197 cm−1 and 1109 cm−1 was due to the hydrogen sulfur stretching vibration. The weak peak 1025 cm−1 was due to the C-N stretching representing the presence of amines. The peaks at 859 cm−1, 652 cm−1, 581 cm−1, and 451 cm−1 are due to the presence of C-S stretching vibration of hydrogen sulfide. The band at 3236 cm−1 was strongly attributed to the O–H stretching vibration of alcohols, phenols, and carboxylic acids and the N–H stretching vibration of the amines and amides. The small band at 1674 cm−1 showed the presence of CC aromatic rings. The band at 1430 cm−1 is generally attributed to the C–O asymmetric stretching, O–H deformation, C–O–H deformation of the carboxyl groups, and symmetric stretching of the COO– ions. The band at 1189 cm−1 may be attributed to the hydrogen sulfur stretching vibration. The sharp curve at 1084 cm−1 relatively defined the S-O stretch of inorganic sulfates. The 990 cm−1, 750 cm−1, and 612 cm−1 peaks showed the presence of derivatives like phenols, carbon dioxide, and straight-chain hydrocarbons. The functional groups confirm the degradation of phenolic compounds, aromatic hydrocarbons, and CO2 evolution reaction. The detection of more functional groups and compounds in the treated sample is due to the formation of byproducts from the degradation of higher molecular weight organic substances. The detection of similar functional groups is identified at 2 h and 3 h of treatment which shows that the functional groups don’t change after 30 min of treatment. The NO2, CO amide group, and C–N stretching were found in the raw leachate which is not detected in the treated sample indicating the reduction nitrate group.

The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectroscopy for the dissolved organic matter (DOM) removal for raw leachate and after electrofenton at 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 60 min, and 120 min was illustrated in Fig. 4 . The EEM spectra can be divided into five regions based on their excitation-emission wavelength categorizing DOM into different types as given in Table ST2
[53]. In raw leachate, the dense fluorescence intensity was found. The presence of more compounds was in Region V (Humic acid-like compounds) and fewer compounds were present in Region III (Hydrophobic acid, fulvic acid-like compounds), Region IV (Protein and soluble microbial byproduct-like compounds), and Region II aromatic proteins (such as tyrosine and tryptophan). No compounds were present in Region I. After 10 min of electrolysis, the Region II and Region IV compounds were fully removed and the majority of Region III, as well as Region V, were removed. After 20 min, only negligible compounds were present in Region V and Region III which was also completely removed at 30 min. The results were also in correlation with the SUVA value. A higher SUVA value denotes a large presence of aromaticity in wastewater. The SUVA value has decreased from 0.258 to 0.199 at optimized treatment conditions [54]. The fenton experiment was carried out at 3 pH, 80 mM Fe2+, 1.8 g/L H2O2, 60 rpm, and 240 min of reaction time in which the DOM was not removed as shown in Fig. S3
(e). Hence, electrofenton has proved to be more effective in degrading DOM than fenton alone. The DOM removal was also illustrated for different electrofenton experiments like zero iron dosage, zero Fe2+ dosage, variation of pH, and only pH 3 of leachate in Fig. 3 (d),
S1, S2, and S3 .

To analyze the nature of organic matter, toxicity, and byproducts in raw and treated leachate at optimized conditions, the GC–MS analysis was done. In the raw sample, 62 organic compounds were present which included 12 amines, 11 alkanes, 9 carboxylic acids, 8 alcohols, 8 amides, 8 ketones, 3 esters, 1 aldehyde, 1 ether, and 1 silane. The optimized condition-treated sample has 86 organic compounds that include 20 amines, 17 acids, 14 alkanes, 13 alcohols, 8 ketones, 8 amides, 3 aldehydes, 2 esters, and 1 nitrile with matching of more than 80 %. The number of carboxylic acid group compounds increases after treatment is in agreement with the EEM results wherein the Region III and V contribute to humic and fulvic-like compounds are confirmed by Fig. 4 (b) and (c) and FTIR broadband spectra at 3290 cm−1. The molecular structure of some detected compounds is represented in Tables ST5 and ST6 . The raw leachate has higher molecular weight humic substances which cannot be determined by GC–MS. After treatment, due to the degradation of higher molecular weight substances into lower molecular weight, the formation of intermediates and byproducts occurs leading to more detection of compounds [45]. This can also be confirmed by the FTIR results.

Benzeneethanamine is produced during food processing such as fermenting cocoa to produce chocolates. Benzeneethanamine,5-benzyloxy-3-fluoro-beta (tr = 24.2), listed as toxic by EPA [63] was found in raw leachate which was not detected in the treated sample. Butanamide, (tr = 4.08), propanamide, (tr = 4.12), ethanone,1-(3-ethyloxiranyl), (tr = 6.75), cis-2,3-epoxyheptane, (tr = 6.8), piperazine,1,4-dimethyl, (tr = 6.9), butanamine, n-methyl-n-nitro, (tr = 4.14), oxalic acid isohexyl pentyl ester (tr = 6.66) were all found in raw leachate which are toxic according to NCBI [62]. All compounds were removed after 30 min of electrooxidation except piperazine,1,4-dimethyl, butanamide, and n-methyl-n-nitro which were removed after 120 min. Butanamide is a food flavoring agent, propanamide derivatives are used in pharma industries and agricultural pesticides, epoxides have a variety of applications from detergents to glue manufacturing, and Piperazines are used in the manufacture of plastics, brake fluids, pesticides, and other industrial materials.

Oxalic acid is used in food additives, pharma industries, battery production, and other industrial applications. In raw leachate, toxins such as pentanoic acid,2-(aminooxy), (tr = 4.24), and 4 h-pyran-4-one,2,3-dihydro-3,5-dihydroxy-6-methyl (tr = 26.43) were present which are hazardous to the aquatic environment as a long-term hazard along with skin, eye, lungs irritation. Pentanoic acid,2-(aminooxy) was removed after 30 min of treatment, and 4 h-pyran-4-one was removed after 240 min of treatment. Pentanoic acid is used in herbicides, food additives, and perfume manufacturing industries and pyran-4-one has a lot of pharmaceutical and dye synthesis applications. Carcinogens such as N-ethyl-n'-nitroguanidine, (tr = 4.17), chloroaniline-5-sulfonic acid, (tr = 31.9,1), and possible carcinogen 5-(2-aminopropyl)-2-methyl phenol (tr = 4.46), were found in raw leachate and not detected after 30 min. Nitroguanidine is a pharmaceutical, pesticide industry intermediate used in anticorrosivephosphatecoatings.

The possible mechanism of nitroguanidine degradation is proposed in Fig. 6 . The byproduct formation mechanism is in agreement with the previous publication [55]. Chloroaniline is a chemical found in dye, pharmaceuticals, agricultural products, and furnishing materials like hammocks, area rugs, mattresses, etc., Methyl phenol is detected in consumer products like automotive care products, paints, and coatings. Glutaraldehyde, (tr = 19.67), is acutely toxic and hazardous to the aquatic environment and was found as intermediate in the 30-minute treated sample, but not detected after 120 min or 240 min of electrooxidation.

Hence, most of the toxins, and carcinogens were degraded and some intermediate toxins were formed which also oxidized with an increase in electrooxidation time. Thus, electrofenton with Stainless steel electrodes was proved to be efficient in removing major contaminants. The comparison of the Stainless steel electrode for COD removal with various electrooxidation experiments is given in Table 2
. The compounds detected by GC–MS analysis were categorized into acids, alkalis, etc., with their O/C, H/C, N/C ratios, and CHON molecular weight which were represented in the van Krevelen diagram as represented in Fig. 5
&
Fig. S4 . The details explanation is given in the supplementary information.

The nature of compounds present in the DOM has been characterized by various UV ratios and SUVA values are given in supplementary information. Table ST3 shows the various values obtained for raw, 10-minute, 20-minute, and 30-minute treated samples for optimum dosage. The various UV value ratios, SUVA values, and their significance are given in the supplementary information.

The results of the heavy metal analysis are given in supplementary information
Table ST4 . The explanation of detection and change in metal concentration during the electrofenton treatment is given in the supplementary material.

Cost analysis mainly determines the suitability of a technology for choosing it as an alternative to existing technology. The energy consumption is the important factor in determining the overall cost of treatment. In this electrofenton treatment of leachate, at the optimized conditions, the energy consumption was evaluated to be 0.26 KWh/Kg COD.

The electrofenton reaction is assumed to follow the pseudo-first-order kinetic model because the TOC and UV254 concentration decreases linearly with an increase in time for the initial 30 min. The kinetic reactions of the electrooxidation were examined based on the TOC (k = 0.076179) and UV254 (k = 0.05108) concentrations which are fitting to the pseudo-first-order kinetic given in Fig. 3 (c). Thus, the reaction shows the pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting high linear regression (R2 > 0.98) for both the TOC and UV254 degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, optical, and structural properties of S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The sludge produced from the electrofenton experiment is converted into quantum dots and its different properties are discussed below. The morphology of S-CQDs obtained using TEM is shown in Fig. 8
(a). The S-CQDs are spherical with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm showing the presence of graphitic carbon [56,57]. The Gaussian fitting curve in Fig. 8 (b) confirms that the S-CQDs are uniformly distributed with an average particle size of 2.3 nm. Due to its nature of less particle size and more surface area with attached many heterogeneous bonding groups, it gets agglomerated soon as shown in Fig. 8(a). The S-CQDs give green color fluorescence under UV light as shown in Fig. 7. The XRD pattern of the S-CQDs, is given in Fig. 9
(a). The broad diffraction peaks at 23° confirm the presence of an amorphous carbon structure [23,24,58]. The FTIR pattern is represented in Fig. 9 (b). The band at 3220 cm−1 represents the O–H stretching. The small band at 2090 cm−1 may be due to the triple bond CC or CN stretching vibration. The tiny peak at 1870 cm−1 is due to the stretching of double bond CC or CO or CN. the peak at 1650 cm−1 alkenes CC stretch or CO stretching vibration of carboxylic acid or amide group. The peak at 1420 cm−1 is due to the NO stretch indicating the presence of nitrate functional groups. The peak at 1080 cm−1 SO stretch represents the presence of the inorganic sulfates functional group. The band at 604 cm−1 is owing to C-S stretching vibration showing the presence of hydrogen sulfides functional group. The presence of these functional groups indicates that the synthesized S-CQDs are hydrophilic [59,60]. The UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy is given in Fig. 9 (c). The absorption band at 310 nm denotes the presence of transition of the polycyclic aromatic CO bonds in dots in agreement with the FTIR results [28]. The band gap energy was determined using the equation number 12 as mentioned above [41]. The wavelength λ was determined from the UV–Vis curve as shown in Fig. 9 (c). The wavelength was found to be 310 nm and the corresponding band gap energy determined was 4 eV. This higher band gap reveals the suitability of the synthesized S-CQDs for higher voltage material applications. The PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths with a 20 nm increment from 240 to 360 nm are shown in Fig. 9 (d). When an excitation wavelength was more than 320 nm, the emission intensities were reduced and exhibited red shift again confirms the existence of heteroatoms with different emissive sites in the quantum dots. From the PL and UV–Vis values the quantum yield was calculated to be 38.4 %. This yield is higher compared to the quantum yield of green synthesis of quantum dots from natural plants, fruits, etc., [22].

The S-CQDs applied for fingerprint development on normal paper with and without UV–Vis light fluorescence digital photography was shown in Fig. 10 . The fingerprint development is done on white paper as shown in Fig. 10 (a) and the fingerprint impression with S-CQDs black powder as per the procedure explained kept in the white paper is shown in Fig. 10 (b). The fingerprint visibility under UV-365 nm is shown in Fig. 10 (c). The fingerprint development without changing the original contour of the latent fingerprint is an important factor in determining the accuracy of the process. The quality is usually verified in three ways [36]. The first way is the maintenance of the overall ridge flow pattern which is excellently matching with the original fingerprint as shown in Fig. 10 (b). The second way is the points of identification or minutiae namely, bifurcations (3, 4), ridges (1, 2) and dots (5, 6) are matching as shown in Fig. 10 (d) as three specific areas with point numbers under normal and UV light. The consistent edge pattern is the third quality check which is very similar to the black powder and the UV light fingerprint. Thus, the S-CQDs powder fingerprint development is successful in meeting the quality of standard fingerprint development in all ways. The synthesized S-CQDs liquid is used as paint without any addition of solvent or other chemicals. An Indian rangoli is painted on a normal white paper shown in Fig. 10 (d) painting under normal light and 10(e) fluorescence painting under UV light. The attractive fluorescence establishes that the synthesized quantum dots are well-suited for fluorescent painting applications. For three months, the fingerprint and fluorescent painting samples are appropriately wrapped and maintained in airtight packages. After three months, the fluorescence of the CQDs was tested for stability under UV light. The fact that both the fingerprint and the fluorescent painting show the same level of fluorescence after 3 months demonstrates the dots' stability for the application. This is depicted in Fig. S6
. The comparison graph for the PL intensity ratio [26], for the synthesized quantum dots stored in a refrigerator under the dark at 4 °C and under room temperature & light is given in Fig. S7
. Here P0 is the initial PL and P is the PL after n number of days. The graph depicts that the PL is almost stable under proper storage conditions and reduced little under room atmosphere.

In this study, parameters like reaction time, pH, voltage, distance between the electrodes, Fe2+ dosage, aeration, and H2O2 dosage were optimized. The parameters like TOC, BOD, UV254, 3dEEM, COD, and NH4-N show the degradation of organic compounds in leachate. The GC–MS results were used to assess the removal of carcinogens and toxins in raw leachate, and the nature of intermediates, and byproducts formed during the electrofenton reaction. The intermediates and the byproducts are not toxic which reveals that the electrofenton treatment is safe for the environment. The van Krevelen diagram of GC–MS detected compounds was used for their respective O/C, H/C, and N/C ratios visualizing DOM molecular transformation. The CHNO parameter graph was also classified from the GC–MS results and compared with the raw and treated samples. The Stainless steel electrodes have been proven to be suitable for the electrooxidation reaction of wastewater treatment. The electron fenton-treated sludge derived from the S-CQDs (10 nm) material was found to be highly efficient in anti-counterfeiting applications under UV light conditions. Thus, the sludge S-CQDs material having a higher quantum yield with higher band gap energy opens its applications to various energy-related commercial products.
